insights: Computer assets exhibit an extreme negative cost-warranty correlation (-0.9990), meaning higher-cost computers consistently receive significantly shorter warranties—averaging only 363 days despite a mean cost of $3,345—contradicting standard procurement logic where higher-value assets should carry longer warranty coverage.

The Computer category accounts for 45.6% of all assets (228 of 500 records) and drives the overall weak negative cost-warranty correlation (-0.1376), while all other categories show near-zero or expected positive relationships, indicating the discrepancy is concentrated and materially distorts enterprise-level warranty alignment.

The extreme negative correlation in the Computer category is systematic across all 7 departments (correlations ranging from -0.9987 to -1.0000) and all 5 time periods (2023-Q2 to 2024-Q2), confirming it is not attributable to departmental policy, temporal procurement shifts, or data artifacts—but rather reflects a structural vendor- or product-level warranty design.

Servers and Web Servers—though fewer in volume—show the strongest alignment between cost and warranty duration (mean cost ~$8,000; mean warranty 740–815 days), highlighting that proper cost-warranty proportionality is achievable within the same procurement ecosystem and underscoring the anomaly’s specificity to computer hardware sourcing.

Customer Support and Sales departments account for 66% of all computer purchases (151 of 228), yet their cost-warranty correlations (-0.9989 and -0.9992) are statistically indistinguishable from smaller departments, confirming that procurement volume or departmental focus does not explain the pattern—only vendor contract terms or bundled product configurations can plausibly account for it.

Warranty durations across all assets are valid and well-distributed (90–1096 days; mean 585 days), confirming data quality is sufficient to treat the Computer category anomaly as a real operational discrepancy—not a data artifact—thereby elevating its priority for procurement strategy review.
summary: The analysis reveals a critical misalignment in procurement strategy: computer assets—representing nearly half of all purchased assets—systematically violate the expected positive relationship between cost and warranty duration, with higher-cost units receiving dramatically shorter coverage (mean 363 days). This extreme negative correlation (-0.9990) is consistent across all departments and time periods, ruling out internal policy or timing as causes and pointing decisively to vendor-level warranty structures or product bundling practices. In contrast, servers and web servers demonstrate appropriate cost-warranty proportionality, proving the enterprise’s procurement framework can support aligned terms when applied to other asset classes. This discrepancy poses tangible financial risk—shorter warranties on high-cost computers increase long-term maintenance liability—and signals a need to renegotiate computer-specific vendor contracts or introduce category-level warranty minimums. The next priority is to audit vendor agreements and product SKUs for computers to isolate whether the anomaly stems from standardized OEM terms, channel-specific configurations, or unmanaged third-party reseller policies.
